coredata.dc:description: Algae, particularly Chlorella vulgaris, present a novel, sustainable source for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with unique properties. This study introduces an eco-friendly synthesis method using a hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 12 h, yielding stable, spherical CQDs with an average diameter of 7.19 ± 0.06 nm, composed primarily of carbon and oxygen. The innovation consists on employing microalgal biomass for the production of CQDs, hence obviating the necessity for additional chemicals or passivating agents. The CQDs exhibit a fluorescence peak at 416 nm upon excitation at 241 nm, with UV–visible spectra showing π→π∗ and n→π∗ transition at 241 nm and 356 nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM analysis reveals a crystalline structure with a 0.21 nm interlayer spacing, confirmed by lattice fringes, and a quantum yield of 41.24%, indicating efficient photoluminescence. The CQDs demonstrate strong antimicrobial activity against pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, with significant inhibition of quorum sensing like twitching and swarming and bacterial motilities. Furthermore, the CQDs achieve a 91.47% degradation of malachite green dye, underscoring their potential in environmental remediation. This study highlights the dual applications of Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs in health and environmental contexts, presenting a sustainable and innovative approach for nanomaterial synthesis.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Support by the Department of Food Technology and Nutrition, Lovely Professional University is gratefully acknowledged.

Swarming motility refers to the rapid, coordinated movement of bacterial cells across a solid surface. This type of motility is typically associated with the production of surface-active molecules and is often observed on semi-solid agar media. Swarming allows bacteria to colonize new environments and is influenced by nutrient availability and surface hardness. The effect of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs on swarming motility was assessed by incorporating various compound concentrations into semi-solid agar media. During swarming motility, bacteria typically grow as tendrils extending from the inoculation site, with widespread branching observed in the control plates. However, the diameter of the swarming zone significantly decreased in the presence of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. The results indicated a dramatic reduction in the swarming zone diameter from 7.57 \u00B1 0.28 cm (Control) to 3.91 \u00B1 0.23 cm, 2.64 \u00B1 0.21 cm, 1.82 \u00B1 0.19 and 1.09 \u00B1 0.16 cm at concentrations of 300, 600, 900, and 1200 \u03BCl/mL, respectively (p < 0.001) (see Table 2). Given that swarming motility is dependent on bacterial cell density, the observed reduction in motility zone diameter likely results from the bactericidal effect of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs in a dose-dependent manner. The impaired swarming motility following treatment with Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs is further supported by its inhibitory impact on rhamnolipid production.Support by the Department of Central Instrumentation facility, Lovely Professional University is gratefully acknowledged and The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSPD2024R940), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Algae, particularly Chlorella vulgaris, present a novel, sustainable source for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with unique properties. This study introduces an eco-friendly synthesis method using a hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 12 h, yielding stable, spherical CQDs with an average diameter of 7.19 ± 0.06 nm, composed primarily of carbon and oxygen. The innovation consists on employing microalgal biomass for the production of CQDs, hence obviating the necessity for additional chemicals or passivating agents. The CQDs exhibit a fluorescence peak at 416 nm upon excitation at 241 nm, with UV–visible spectra showing π→π∗ and n→π∗ transition at 241 nm and 356 nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM analysis reveals a crystalline structure with a 0.21 nm interlayer spacing, confirmed by lattice fringes, and a quantum yield of 41.24%, indicating efficient photoluminescence. The CQDs demonstrate strong antimicrobial activity against pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, with significant inhibition of quorum sensing like twitching and swarming and bacterial motilities. Furthermore, the CQDs achieve a 91.47% degradation of malachite green dye, underscoring their potential in environmental remediation. This study highlights the dual applications of Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs in health and environmental contexts, presenting a sustainable and innovative approach for nanomaterial synthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, are distinguished by their carbonaceous core, which combines sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms (Zhang et al., 2024). They are notable for their functional groups, predominantly hydroxyl and carboxyl, which give them excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, and the capacity for straightforward functionalization (S. Chen, Liu, et al., 2024). Moreover, CQDs exhibit redox capabilities and a tendency for electron transfer and storage, making them highly versatile across various applications. CQDs, initially identified in studies of carbon nanotube electrocatalysis, have since found applications in diverse areas including biosensing, environmental monitoring, drug delivery, and energy storage (Y.-J. Ma et al., 2024). Typically, CQDs are synthesized through Top-down or Bottom-up approaches, with the latter gaining preference due to the potential of using sustainable and cost-effective biomass as carbon sources (Saraswat et al., 2024). Microalgal biomass is a compelling choice among biomass sources, attributed to its rich elemental composition and rapid biodegradability. Chlorella vulgaris, a type of microalgae, is particularly promising due to its high content of nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur, which can enhance the doping of CQDs, potentially improving their photoluminescent properties and catalytic performance (Jun-Won & Kim, 2023).

Algal biomass is being utilized more frequently for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) due to its sustainable and environmentally friendly characteristics (Li et al., 2024). Rich in carbon and various organic molecules, algae provide a renewable source that can be harnessed in a low-cost, green synthesis process. The inherent properties of algae, such as high growth rates and abundant biomass yield, make it a scalable option (Chai et al., 2024). Furthermore, the natural presence of functional groups in algae, like amino acids and fatty acids, facilitates the doping of CQDs, enhancing their optical and electronic properties. Chlorella vulgaris was chosen as a carbon source for CQD synthesis due to its high content of homo-polysaccharides, particularly rich in galactose, which contributes to the formation of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the CQD surface. These functional groups are critical for enhancing the photoluminescent and electronic properties of the CQDs, making them highly effective for applications in bioimaging and sensing (Dong et al., 2024). Additionally, Chlorella vulgaris offers an environmentally friendly alternative, as its natural composition minimizes the need for harsh chemicals, aligning with green chemistry principles. Unlike conventional CQD synthesis that often relies on harsh chemicals, utilizing Chlorella vulgaris involves milder conditions and non-toxic solvents, primarily water, minimizing environmental impact (Yadav et al., 2024). Chlorella vulgaris, a species of green microalgae, has garnered significant attention for the CQDs due to its unique composition and environmental benefits. This algae is rich in carbon, nitrogen, and other essential nutrients, making it an ideal precursor for producing high-quality CQDs (Flores et al., 2024). The high nitrogen content is significant as it naturally dopes carbon dots, improving their photoluminescent properties, which are crucial for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and photocatalysis. One of the primary reasons for choosing Chlorella vulgaris is its rapid growth rate and high biomass yield, which ensure a sustainable and scalable source of raw material for CQD synthesis (Song et al., 2024).

Moreover, the hydrothermal method of deriving CQDs from Chlorella vulgaris is straightforward and energy-efficient (X. Ma & Li, 2024). This process not only facilitates the extraction of carbonaceous materials but also allows the functional groups inherent in the algal biomass to impart stability and water solubility to the CQDs (İzgi & Sait, 2024). Consequently, the synthesized CQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, making them appropriate for biological applications. The synthesis of CQDs from Chlorella vulgaris involves the hydrothermal method, which is advantageous for its simplicity (Ferjani et al., 2024). This method employs water as the reaction medium and utilizes the intrinsic properties of the biomass, thus avoiding the use of harmful chemical reagents. The potential of Chlorella vulgaris in this synthesis lies in its rapid growth rate and high efficiency in photosynthesis, which makes it an abundant, renewable resource ideal for sustainable nanomaterial production (Onat et al., 2024).

The photocatalytic degradation of dyes using CQDs derived from algal biomass presents a promising approach for environmental remediation, particularly in the treatment of wastewater contaminated with synthetic dyes like Malachite Green (Lanjwani et al., 2024). Algal biomass-CQDs derived from algal biomass exhibit distinctive catalytic properties attributed to their high surface area and the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, which facilitate effective interactions with organic dye molecules. When applied to the photocatalytic degradation of Malachite Green, these CQDs act as photocatalysts under light irradiation (Ahmed et al., 2024). During the process, reactive oxygen species (ROS) like hydroxyl radicals, superoxide anions, and hydrogen peroxide are generated. These ROS are potent oxidizers that break down complex dye molecules into less harmful or harmless compounds (Nagaraja et al., 2024). This reaction not only reduces the visual and ecological impact of dyes in wastewater but also diminishes their toxicity, contributing to safer water bodies. Moreover, the use of algal biomass as a precursor for CQD synthesis aligns with green chemistry principles, promoting the utilization of renewable resources and reducing chemical waste (Kotian et al., 2024). Thus, the application of algal biomass-derived CQDs in the photocatalytic degradation of Malachite Green not only demonstrates a high degree of dye reduction but also represents a step forward in the sustainable treatment of polluted environments (Nayak et al., 2024).

Using CQDs derived from algal biomass in antimicrobial applications represents a promising path in the fight against microbial pathogens (Lv et al., 2024). These CQDs, synthesized from renewable algal sources synthesized CQDs, exhibit significant antimicrobial properties owing to their unique surface chemistry and small size. Their high surface-to-volume ratio enables effective interactions with microbial cells, disrupting cell membranes and inducing oxidative stress by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Sun et al., 2024). Algal-derived CQDs, possess intrinsic characteristics such as low toxicity, making them well-suited for biomedical applications (Deb & Chowdhury, 2023). They exhibit the potential to target various pathogens, including bacteria, fungi, and viruses. The functional groups present on the surface of these CQDs, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups enhance their ability to interact with and penetrate microbial cell walls, thereby increasing their efficacy as antimicrobial agents (Khare, 2024).

Previous research has investigated the utilization of algal biomass as a precursor for synthesizing CQDs through hydrothermal methods (He et al., 2024). Nonetheless, comprehensive analyses utilizing techniques such as energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering, and differential scanning calorimetry were not adequately discussed. Furthermore, data regarding anti-quorum activity, microbial inhibition kinetics, and the efficacy of photocatalytic dye degradation under UV–visible light were insufficient (Aslam et al., 2023). This research aims to shift the focus towards utilizing Chlorella vulgaris, a type of green algae, for the eco-friendly production of CQDs through hydrothermal processes, which is a novel approach in green chemistry. This study aims to assess the thermal stability of algae-derived CQDs, their efficacy in inhibiting microbial growth, and their role in mitigating diseases caused by pathogenic microorganisms through the inhibition of virulence factors associated with quorum sensing and biofilm formation, as well as their ability to degrade malachite green dye. The synthesis process emphasizes waste valorization and sustainable practices. The characterization of these CQDs will focus on their structural, optical, and chemical properties, as well as their potential as antimicrobial agents against foodborne pathogens, thereby enhancing food safety. Additionally, this research will assess the CQD's photocatalytic performance in breaking down synthetic malachite green dye, highlighting their application in environmental remediation. Overall, the conversion of Chlorella vulgaris into CQDs via hydrothermal methods marks a significant contribution to green chemistry, addressing both public health and environmental challenges.

This study utilized algal biomass (Chlorella vulgaris) sourced from ponds at Lovely Professional University, Jalandhar, Punjab, India. The freshly collected microalgae were filtered using double-layered muslin cloth and then sealed in airtight glass containers, which were stored under refrigerated conditions (4–7 °C) until they were required for experiments. All reagents used were of analytical grade. Ethanol (99.5% purity), sodium hydroxide (99% purity), PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene) syringe filters with a pore size of 0.45 μm, syringes, and dialysis membranes (3 KDa) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Malachite green dye (99%) was obtained from Loba Chemie Ltd., Mumbai, India. The microbial strains employed to assess antimicrobial efficacy, comprising Gram-negative bacteria such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Salmonella typhi (S. typhi), and Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae) and Gram-positive bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were sourced from the Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC) at the Institute of Microbial Technology in Chandigarh, India. Experiments utilized ultrapure water procured from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from Chlorella vulgaris
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from Chlorella vulgaris using a straightforward hydrothermal method as described by Irwan (2024). To achieve a uniform dispersion, 20 ml of purified Chlorella vulgaris was vigorously stirred with 80 ml of Milli-Q water at 500 rpm using a magnetic stirrer (IKA Works GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) for 30 min at 27 °C. Chlorella vulgaris was purified by harvesting and washing with Milli-Q water prior to resuspension for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. The prepared solution was subsequently transferred to a 100 ml capacity Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Techinstro, Nagpur, India), and placed at 200 °C for 10 h in a hot air oven. After heating, the resulting greenish-yellow solution was drawn into a 10 ml syringe and centrifuged at 5500×g for 30 min using a centrifuge (Sorvall™ Legend™ X1 Centrifuge, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The solution was further purified through a 3 KDa dialysis membrane to remove any impurities or larger particles. The clear, fluorescent greenish-yellow solution was purified and transferred into quartz vials using a glass syringe to prevent additional reactions. Subsequently, fabricated CQDs were stored under 4–7 °C until further utilization. In the sample preparation stage, the CQDs solution underwent filtration using a 0.45 μm syringe filter to facilitate additional characterization and various applications.

In this equation, Φ signifies the comparative quantum yield of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs relative to that of quinine sulfate, a reference compound (Φr) with a concentration of 0.1 mg/L in 0.1 M H2SO4 and an excitation wavelength of 360 nm "I" signifies the emission intensity derived from the emission spectrum with a 360 nm excitation. "A" is the UV–visible light absorbance at 360 nm, maintained below 0.05 in the 10 mm quartz cell to prevent re-absorption effects. The solvent's refractive index (η) was noted as 1.33.

The effectiveness of Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs in photocatalytic degradation was evaluated using malachite green dye, following a method adapted from (Raja et al., 2023; Roopan et al., 2024) with minor modifications. The experiment evaluated the dye's degradation under UV–visible light exposure using a 200 W LED lamp in the presence of the catalyst. The dye-catalyst mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring at 250 rpm for 30 min in the absence of light to facilitate adsorption-desorption. The test setup comprised 100 ml of a 10 ppm malachite green dye solution and 15 ml of the Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQD catalyst. Following the equilibration, the suspension was illuminated with UV–visible light while stirring continued for 30–40 min. A control experiment with just the diluted dye solution was also conducted under similar conditions but without the catalyst to serve as a baseline. Throughout the degradation process, 2 ml samples were extracted at predetermined intervals (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min) and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then analyzed using a spectrophotometer to determine the absorption spectra between 400 nm and 800 nm. The percentage degradation of malachite green was determined using the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of reactive oxygen species in photodegradation process
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in degrading Malachite green (10 ppm) dye was evaluated following the experimental procedure outlined by Meiyazhagan et al. (2021) with certain modifications. Briefly, the tests were conducted using the same photocatalytic reaction methodology employed for Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs (100 mL dye solution, 10 mL Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs, for a specific time under UV–visible light irradiation). To identify the significant and minor contributing photo-active species scavenger tests were performed. Various scavengers, including tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ), silver nitrate, and potassium iodide (KI), were introduced into the photocatalytic system to eliminate hydroxyl radicals, holes, superoxide radicals, free electrons, and hydrogen peroxide, respectively.

The effectiveness of CQDs extracted from Chlorella vulgaris algae against bacteria including S. aureus, K. pneumonia, S. typhi, and P. aeruginosa was evaluated. A modified well diffusion method, following the guidelines highlighted by Suresh et al. (2023), was employed in triplicate. To maintain sterility, the petri dishes and samples underwent autoclaving at 120 °C for 30 min before conducting the antimicrobial assays. Subsequently, a freshly prepared bacterial inoculum was uniformly distributed onto nutrient agar surfaces using a sterilized cotton swab. The test solution was prepared by diluting the stock solution of CQDs derived from algal biomass with sterile Milli-Q water. A concentration of 0.01 μg/ml of the Chlorella vulgaris-carbon dots was added to wells previously made in the agar. Alongside the CQDs, chloramphenicol served as the positive control and DMSO as the negative control, both of which were also added to respective wells. The inoculated plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, the zones of inhibition were measured. Additionally, the broth microdilution method was employed to ascertain the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of the synthesized CQDs against the tested bacterial strains. Serial dilutions of the CQDs were prepared in nutrient broth, dispensed into microtiter plates, and then the MIC and MBC results were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time kill kinetic analysis of Chlorella vulgaris-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antimicrobial properties of CQDs obtained from Chlorella vulgaris against pathogenic bacteria, a time-kill kinetic study was conducted using the approach outlined by (Yan et al., 2019). Müller-Hinton broth, commonly used in antimicrobial susceptibility tests, served as the growth medium for this analysis. During the study, various concentrations of algal biomass-derived CQDs were poured into the bacterial cultures at specific time intervals. The bacterial populations were subsequently measured using viable cell count techniques. This involved measuring the log CFU/mL (logarithm of colony-forming units per milliliter) at specific time points: initial at 0 h, then 18 h, and later stages (24 h and 48 h) of interaction. The data collected from this time kill kinetic, study present critical insights into the effectiveness of algal biomass-derived CQDs as antimicrobial agents, furthering their potential use in nanomaterial-based therapies against bacterial pathogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-quorum sensing studies motility assay of Chlorella vulgaris-CQD

ce:section.ce:para: The impact of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs on the twitching motility of P. aeruginosa was assessed using a method detailed by Omran et al. (2024). The twitching medium was prepared with Lysogeny broth containing 1% Bactoagar (HiMedia, India). After sterilization, Chlorella vulgaris-CQD at concentrations of 300, 600, 900, and 1200 μl/mL was incorporated into the agar medium, which was then poured into petri dishes and allowed to air dry for 1 h. Under sterile conditions, a bacterial culture with an optical density (OD600) of 0.8 was stab-inoculated at the center of the petri dishes. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Following incubation, the diameter of the twitching motility zone was measured.

The effect of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs on the swarming motility of Pseudomonas aeruginosa has been assessed using a method detailed by Sarangi et al. (2024). The swarming medium was prepared using Lysogeny broth supplemented with 0.5% Bactoagar. After the medium was sterilized, it was mixed with Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs at concentrations of 300, 600, 900, and 1200 μl/mL, then poured into petri dishes and air dried. Control plates contained phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) instead of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. A bacterial culture with an optical density (OD600) of 0.8 was aseptically inoculated at the center of the petri dishes. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Following incubation, the diameter of the swarming zones originating from the inoculation point was measured to evaluate the swarming motility.

All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses were performed using ANOVA in Microsoft 365 ( Washington, USA), with a significance set at p < 0.05. Graphs and data illustrations were generated utilizing Origin Pro 9.0 software (OriginLab Corporation, Massachusetts, USA).

This research focuses on the fabrication of carbon dots, where these nanoparticles are synthesized using Chlorella vulgaris, a readily accessible, cost-effective, and eco-friendly algal biomass. This approach influences the natural abundance and renewable nature of Chlorella vulgaris to produce carbon quantum dots.

This study investigates the synthesis of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs, selected for its inherent carboxyl groups crucial in carbon bonding on the CQD surfaces. The process involves a hydrothermal method conducted at 200 °C for 12 h, where Milli-Q water acts as the solvent. During this hydrothermal reaction, carbon-based materials from Chlorella vulgaris react under elevated temperatures, prompting an oxidation process that enhances their fluorescence capabilities. The procedure includes the application of high pressure and temperature in a hydrothermal reactor, encouraging interactions between the organic precursors and water. The environment inside the reactor reaches pressures up to 3 MPa, and as the temperature rises, the pressure increases, leading to the splitting of water molecules. During hydrolysis, hydrogen ions (H+) and hydroxyl ions (OH−) are produced, inducing repulsion between the carbon layers. Concurrently, hydrogen bonds are formed through interactions with water molecules. As the reaction progresses, the solution containing the CQDs transitions to a light brown color, indicating changes in surface morphology and the formation of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. The presence of hydrophilic groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and carbonyl groups within the CQDs enhances their dispersion in aqueous environments, ideal for applications such as wastewater treatment. The lone electrons on the hydrogen atoms of surface functional groups like hydroxyl and amino groups can readily attract the more electronegative oxygen atoms. This facilitates the formation of hydrogen bonds, which enhance the stability and solubility of CQDs in aqueous environments, further improving their applicability in bioimaging and sensing. This mechanism promotes the self-assembly of the CQDs into nanoparticle-sized structures and potentially porous spheres during hydrothermal synthesis. The findings from this study align with the outcomes by (Singh et al., 2019), which used a precursor, Dunaliella salina biomass, and noted a particle size of 4.7 nm via TEM and limited antimicrobial activity.

Fig. 1 A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CQDs solution. In the spectrum, a prominent absorption peak at 356 nm indicates the n-π∗ transition, which involves electron movement from non-bonding to anti-bonding π orbitals. Another significant peak at 241 nm is associated with the transitions in the conjugated C-C structures within the carbon core (Kalpana et al., 2023). Comparatively (Al-Ghamdi et al., 2023), reported an absorption peak at 350 nm in their study, linking it to the π-π∗ transitions typical of aromatic sp2 hybridized structures in CQDs derived from Halimeda opuntia green algae. Additionally, the formation of CQDs was evident from the pale yellow appearance of the solution in natural light and its bright blue fluorescence under UV light exposure, aligning with observations by (Li et al., 2024), who noted similar results were obtained with CQDs synthesized from Eucheuma denticulatum biomass.

Fig. 1B shows the FTIR spectrum which exhibited a pronounced and wide peak at 3243 cm−1, indicative of both asymmetric and symmetric O-H stretching vibrations, a common feature for CQDs are produced via biological routes, as noted in (Zhu et al., 2020). In the 3000–2600 cm−1 range, various peaks were observed, likely due to C-H stretching in aliphatic hydrocarbons or aldehydes. An absorption band was observed at 1674 cm−1, typically associated with the C=O bond in anhydride configurations, and amide groups (-CONH-) showed a characteristic absorption band at 1298 cm−1. Additionally, the absorption bands at 1072 cm−1 and 578 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric C–O–C stretching and C–H bending vibrations from methyl groups.

Data presented in Fig. 1C depict the particle size of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. The results indicate that the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs have an average diameter of 7.19 ± 0.06 nm. Additionally, the Zeta potential of these CQDs, measured after pyrolysis at 500 °C, is depicted in Fig. 1D. The zeta potential value of −32 mV indicates high stability of the CQDs, as supported by (Plácido et al., 2019). These findings suggest pronounced electrostatic repulsion among the similarly charged Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs, outweighing any attractive forces. The images confirm that CQDs with an average size of less than 10 nm were effectively synthesized from Chlorella vulgaris using green synthesis methods.

Fig. 1E presents the HR-TEM images of CQDs derived from Chlorella vulgaris, presented at magnifications of 50 nm. This image illustrates that the CQDs are spherical, exhibit a narrow size distribution (less than 8 nm), and are uniformly dispersed. The height profile measurement of the CQDs is 23.07 nm, and the lattice fringes show an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, indicative of the crystalline structure of the CQDs. This morphological characterization aligns with findings from other studies (Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022).

The thermal stability data for Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs, where the DSC analysis identified a single exothermic peak was displayed in Fig. 1F. This peak, recorded at 106.03 °C, is likely attributable to the evaporation of water molecules within the sample. Notably, no peaks were detected below 101 °C, indicating the thermal robustness of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. The DSC curve further shows that the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs begin and complete their thermal transition at 97.01 °C and 122.41 °C, respectively. Following the findings reported in the study by (Suresh et al., 2023), which is outlined in the thermal degradation of a carbon dot-silver nanocomposite sourced from Chondrococcus hornemanni.

Fig. 1F illustrates the excitation and emission spectra for the CQDs obtained from the green algae Chlorella vulgaris. The examination indicates an excitation peak at 241 nm and an emission peak at 416 nm. The significant fluorescence intensity of these CQDs is likely due to their extensive surface area and quantum confinement effects. These characteristics are similar to the findings of Héctor et al. (2022) in which CQDs produced from pelagic Sargassum as a carbon source showed excitation at 431 and 437.8 nm. The consistent emission across various excitation wavelengths results from the lowest singlet state emissions of the CQDs. According to Vishal et al. (2023), these CQDs predominantly display blue, green, and red emissions, depending on the excitation. This variation in emission is primarily due to the n → π∗ transitions facilitated by non-bonding electrons in the carboxylic and carbonyl groups of the CQDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as a reference material. A concentration of 0.1 mg/mL of the CQD sample prepared with Milli-Q water was used for this calculation. Similarly, the same concentration of the reference material was prepared by dissolving quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 to maintain UV–vis absorbance intensity values of 0.1. Subsequently, fluorescence emission spectra of the concentrated solution of the sample and reference were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The QY of the prepared CQDs was calculated using standard equation (1) and was determined to be 41.24 %.

Fig. 2 A represents the UV–visible spectrum of the malachite green dye upon exposure to CQDs derived from Chlorella vulgaris, following varying durations under UV irradiation. This investigation assesses the dye removal abilities of these algal-derived CQDs utilizing malachite green as a representative pollutant dye under UV illumination. The findings reveal a pronounced photocatalytic effect, with an impressive 91.47 % reduction in the dye concentration after 60 min of UV exposure. Initially, no significant dye degradation was noted, however, once the UV light was introduced to the reaction, the degradation rate of the CQDs rapidly escalated, achieving a 45 % reduction within the first 25 min reaching 91.47 % by the 60 min. Fig. 2B graphically depicts these degradation efficiencies. The efficiency of these Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs in facilitating the degradation of malachite green under UV light is consistent with findings from a comparable investigation conducted by Kowsalya et al. (2022). Their study reported a 71% degradation of malachite green within 3 h employing carbon quantum dots derived from Spirulina platensis.

Fig. 2C depicts the role of ROS in the degradation process of dye as a catalyst in the presence of different scavengers. The photodegradation process was majorly influenced by the presence of KI, BQ, and TBA scavengers which underscore the crucial role of hydrogen peroxide, superoxide radicals, and hydroxy radicals. When KI was utilized as a scavenger in the photocatalytic process, the degradation rate for malachite green dye was observed at 37.60 %. In addition, when BQ was used as the scavenger, the degradation rate observed was 46.66 %. Moreover, in the case of the TBA scavenger, the degradation rate was 51.19 %. In contrast, the presence of holes and free electrons has a minor effect on dye photodegradation. In the presence of EDTA and silver nitrate, the degradation rate was 74.23 and 73.28 % for malachite green dye.

Fig. 3 A and C illustrates the antibacterial effectiveness of CQDs sourced from Chlorella vulgaris against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The standard antibiotic chloramphenicol demonstrated a significantly larger inhibition zone (28.14 ± 0.06 mm), whereas the algal-derived CQDs exhibited considerable inhibitory activity, particularly against Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus (26.78 ± 0.10 mm), surpassing their effect on Gram-negative bacteria. The inhibition zones for, S. typhi, K. pneumoniae, and P. aeruginosa measured, 25.16 ± 0.31, 25.28 ± 0.11 and 25.49 ± 0.23 mm, respectively. Additionally, The MIC and MBC for the synthesized CQDs against these bacterial strains were indicated in Fig. 3B. The MICs recorded were 8.08 ± 0.18, 7.59 ± 0.33, 8.64 ± 0.06, and 5.38 ± 0.05 μl/ml for P. aeruginosa, K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, and S. aureus, respectively, with corresponding MBCs of 5.53 ± 0.10, 4.40 ± 0.12, 5.27 ± 0.26, and 3.64 ± 0.06 μl/ml. The antibacterial properties of these CQDs are attributed to the diverse functional groups present, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which disrupt cellular enzymes and hinder bacterial growth. The extensive π-conjugated structure of the CQDs enhances interaction with bacterial cell walls through electron transfer mechanisms. These Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs could effectively disrupt bacterial growth through multiple mechanisms, such as generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) that damage bacterial cell walls and membranes, and electrostatic interactions that facilitate their attachment to and disruption of bacterial surfaces. However, while their antimicrobial properties are promising, the potential toxicity of these CQDs in contaminated foods must be thoroughly evaluated. Ensuring that their application in food products adheres to safety standards is essential to prevent adverse effects on human health while preserving effectiveness against foodborne pathogens. Reactive oxygen species, particularly hydroxyl radicals, identified through FTIR analysis, are crucial for antimicrobial efficacy. The cationic constituents on the carbon dots facilitate attachment to negatively charged bacterial surfaces, allowing the CQDs to break cell membranes and ultimately cause microbial death. This investigation highlights the potential of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs in inhibiting the growth of pathogens like P. aeruginosa, S. typhi, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus, suggesting their applicability in the food industries. Recent research by Reetu et al. (2023), has highlighted the effectiveness of CQDs which were derived from defatted Coelastrella sp., in generating ROS that actively engage and disrupt Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time kill kinetic analysis of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: This investigation evaluates the antimicrobial characteristics of carbon dots derived from Chlorella vulgaris, focusing on their efficacy against specific pathogens through a time kill kinetic study, highlighted in Table 1 . The measured colony forming unit per mL (CFU/mL) values for S. aureus (7.93 ± 0.38 log CFU/mL), S. typhi (7.71 ± 0.17 log CFU/mL), P. aeruginosa (7.75 ± 0.28 log CFU/mL), and K. pneumoniae (7.64 ± 0.16 log CFU/mL) highlight the substantial bactericidal activity of the carbon dots. The primary mechanism involves the CQDs interacting with the bacterial cell walls, compromising their structural integrity, which may increase membrane permeability and lead to the leakage of cellular contents, concluding in cell death. Furthermore, it is acknowledged that CQDs stimulate the generation of reactive oxygen species within bacterial cells, leading to oxidative stress and subsequent deterioration of vital cellular components such as proteins, DNA, and lipids (Werlin et al., 2011). As the exposure duration extends, the combined effects of membrane disruption and oxidative stress enhance, thus amplifying the bactericidal efficacy of the CQDs. The time kill kinetics studies are crucial for understanding the progressive nature of the antimicrobial action of these algal-derived CQDs, which could be pivotal in optimizing their use in applications such as food safety (Hassani et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-quorum sensing studies motility assay of Chlorella vulgaris-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Quorum sensing is a communication mechanism used by P. aeruginosa to coordinate group behaviors based on population density. It involves the production and detection of signaling molecules called autoinducers. When the concentration of these molecules reaches a threshold, it triggers gene expression changes that regulate various functions such as biofilm formation, virulence factor production, and antibiotic resistance. This process enables P. aeruginosa to adapt to environmental changes, enhance its survival, and establish infections more effectively. Disrupting quorum sensing can reduce the pathogenicity of P. aeruginosa, making it a target for antimicrobial strategies.

Twitching motility involves the extension and retraction of type IV pili, hair-like appendages on the bacterial surface. This movement is characterized by short, intermittent jerks, allowing bacteria to crawl across surfaces. Twitching motility plays a crucial role in biofilm formation and the initial stages of infection. The impact of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQD on twitching motility was assessed by incorporating the sample into agar at concentrations of 300, 600, 900, and 1200 μl/mL. Compared to the control, a notable reduction in the diameter of the twitching zone was observed. Specifically, the twitching zone diameter decreased significantly from 4.71 ± 0.26 cm (Control) to 3.79 ± 0.21 cm at 300 μl/mL, 2.35 ± 0.18 cm at 600 μl/mL, 1.46 ± 0.15 cm at 900 μl/mL, and 0.77 ± 0.11 cm at 1200 μl/mL (p < 0.001) (Table 2 ). These findings indicate that the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs at 1200 μg/mL exerts the highest inhibitory effect on the twitching motility of P. aeruginosa. The reduction in twitching motility could be attributed to bacterial death induced by oxidative stress, which is likely caused by the synthesized CQDs. These CQDs may generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the bacterial cells, leading to cellular damage and impaired motility functions. This oxidative stress disrupts essential cellular processes and structures, significantly inhibiting the twitching activity of P. aeruginosa.

Swarming motility refers to the rapid, coordinated movement of bacterial cells across a solid surface. This type of motility is typically associated with the production of surface-active molecules and is often observed on semi-solid agar media. Swarming allows bacteria to colonize new environments and is influenced by nutrient availability and surface hardness. The effect of Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs on swarming motility was assessed by incorporating various compound concentrations into semi-solid agar media. During swarming motility, bacteria typically grow as tendrils extending from the inoculation site, with widespread branching observed in the control plates. However, the diameter of the swarming zone significantly decreased in the presence of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs. The results indicated a dramatic reduction in the swarming zone diameter from 7.57 ± 0.28 cm (Control) to 3.91 ± 0.23 cm, 2.64 ± 0.21 cm, 1.82 ± 0.19 and 1.09 ± 0.16 cm at concentrations of 300, 600, 900, and 1200 μl/mL, respectively (p < 0.001) (see Table 2). Given that swarming motility is dependent on bacterial cell density, the observed reduction in motility zone diameter likely results from the bactericidal effect of the Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs in a dose-dependent manner. The impaired swarming motility following treatment with Chlorella vulgaris-CQDs is further supported by its inhibitory impact on rhamnolipid production.

In summary, a novel approach was developed to utilize algal biomass to produce CQDs through a hydrothermal process. These synthesized CQDs were characterized by various techniques, including UV–vis, DLS, HR-TEM, FT-IR, DSC, and fluorescence spectrophotometry, to analyze their structure, surface chemistry, and optical properties. The Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs exhibited a uniform spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution of less than 8 nm and an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, as revealed by HR-TEM. The QY was determined to be 41.24%, demonstrating their outstanding optical properties. The biocompatibility of these CQDs was confirmed by cellular uptake studies, where they showed efficient penetration into cells, and their robust two-photon fluorescence capabilities were highlighted by significant brightness under near-infrared excitation. Additionally, the CQDs exhibited minimal toxicity at concentrations up to 100 μg/ml, indicating their potential safety in biological applications. The Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs proved to be highly effective as a selective and sensitive on-off sensor for detecting malachite green, achieving up to 93% dye degradation under visible light within 120 min. Furthermore, anti-quorum sensing studies revealed that the CQDs inhibited bacterial communication by up to 85%, while motility assays showed a significant reduction in twitching motility by 70% and swarming motility by 65%. These results underscore the CQDs' potential in antimicrobial applications. This study indicates the first application of microalgae in this context, providing a simple and eco-friendly approach to synthesizing carbon dots from Chlorella vulgaris biomass, eliminating the necessity for addition chemicals or passivating agents. . The resulting CQDs, with their excellent optical properties and significant fluorescent quantum yield, present promising applications in bioimaging, antimicrobial activity, and photocatalytic dye reduction.

Future research should focus on scaling up the production of Chlorella vulgaris-derived CQDs to enable large-scale synthesis while ensuring that their desirable properties are retained. Additionally, in vivo studies are crucial to further evaluate the biocompatibility and toxicity of these CQDs, paving the way for potential clinical applications in bioimaging and therapy. Understanding the detailed mechanisms behind the CQDs' antimicrobial activity, particularly their impact on bacterial quorum sensing, will provide deeper insights into their effectiveness. Expanding the use of these CQDs to environmental remediation, such as heavy metal detection and removal, could showcase their versatility. Moreover, the integration of these CQDs into multi-functional nanocomposites could enhance their properties, making them suitable for advanced technological applications, including optoelectronics and energy storage. This innovative synthesis technique holds significant potential for broadening the application of porous carbon materials across various industries, offering both environmental and technological advancements.
